title: Renewable Energy Master Plan (REMP) — Final Draft Report
issuer: Energy Commission of Nigeria (ECN) / United Nations Development Programme (UNDP)
key_promises: Establish National Renewable Energy Agency (NREA) as implementation champion
Establish Renewable Energy Fund for incentives, micro-credit and R&D
Deploy 40,000 solar home systems by 2007; 400,000 by 2015; 4,000,000 by 2025
Deploy 300,000 improved woodstoves by 2007; 500,000 by 2015; 1,000,000 by 2025
Install 56 MW new renewable electricity by 2007; 746 MW by 2015; 2,945 MW by 2025
Implement zero-import duty moratorium on renewable energy technologies
Create Power Purchase Agreement framework for small RE producers to sell to grid
Deploy 600 MW small hydropower by 2015; 2,000 MW by 2025
Achieve 5% renewable share of national electricity by 2015; 10% by 2025

consumer_reality.nrea_creation: Never established; no equivalent body until REA became operational ~2015
consumer_reality.solar_home_systems: No national programme; off-grid solar remained donor/NGO-driven until ~2016
consumer_reality.improved_woodstoves: No scaled national programme; rural three-stone fires still dominant in 2020s
consumer_reality.small_hydropower: Negligible installation; under 40 MW operational by 2015 against 600 MW target
consumer_reality.import_duty_moratorium: Never implemented; RE equipment continued to attract import duties
consumer_reality.grid_tie_ppa_framework: No PPA framework for small RE producers until well into 2010s
consumer_reality.rural_electrification: REA non-operational until ~2015; rural RE access minimal in plan period
consumer_reality.fuelwood_crisis: Biomass still 37% of energy demand; 95% of rural energy use; deforestation worsening

notable_finding: The REMP 2005 is the most self-aware document in the series and the most completely ignored. It correctly diagnosed every structural barrier to renewable energy deployment — no institutional champion, no financing, no grid-tie mechanism, no quality standards, no import duty relief — and proposed solutions to all of them. The proposed National Renewable Energy Agency (NREA), the critical implementation body, was never created. Woodstove deployment — the cheapest, most immediately impactful intervention for rural consumers — was planned at 300,000 units by 2007 with existing technology from SERC Sokoto; zero scaled programme followed. The document's 2007 electricity targets were deliberately minimal (0.8% of grid) — the authors were already hedging against a system they knew could not deliver.
targets_met: None of the quantitative 2007 or 2015 targets were met within their timeframes
Document successfully mapped Nigeria's renewable resource base
Provided framework later partially adopted by REA post-2015
targets_missed: PPA grid-tie framework for small producers — not created in plan period
links_to_next: REPG 2006 must address the institutional gap — no NREA, no implementation body
The fuelwood/biomass crisis (37% of energy demand, 95% rural) is unaddressed and deepening
Off-grid gap: 100 million Nigerians without electricity at time of REMP launch — figure worsening with population growth
2008 gas flaring deadline (NEP 2003) still approaching; gas-to-power remains the blocking constraint on grid expansion that RE off-grid was supposed to partially compensate for
10,000 MW by 2007 grid target (NEEDS 2004) now structurally impossible — REMP's 0.8% RE share by 2007 was premised on 7,000 MW total grid, itself wildly optimistic
Import duty on RE equipment is a consumer cost passed directly to solar PV buyers — pricing out rural households
